question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
example_answers: In California, public records requests are governed by the California Public Records Act (CPRA). Agencies are required to respond to a request for records within 10 days. This response must either notify the requester that the request has been received and is being processed, deny the request, or notify the requester that certain portions of the request are exempt from disclosure. The 10-day period is for an initial response/determination, not necessarily for the full production of all records, which may take longer depending on the complexity of the request and extensions allowed under the law. || In California, public records requests are governed by the California Public Records Act (CPRA). Under the CPRA, a public agency must notify the requester in writing within 10 days of receiving the request, stating whether the request has been granted, denied, or if a clarification is needed. The agency then has 10 days from that notification to determine whether to comply with the request, though they may seek an extension of time if they demonstrate substantial compelling reasons. || In California, public records requests are governed by the California Public Records Act (CPRA). Under the CPRA, a public agency must respond to a request within 10 days. This response must either notify the requester that the request has been received and will be processed, or notify the requester that the request is being denied in whole or in part. This 10-day response is not a deadline for the actual production of records, but rather a deadline for an initial determination.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
example_answers: Under the New York Freedom of Information Law (FOIL), agencies are required to respond to public records requests within five business days. In this response, the agency must either grant the request, deny it, or provide a date when the request will be granted or denied. If a request is denied, the agency must provide the specific reason for the denial. || Under the New York Freedom of Information Law (FOIL), agencies are required to respond to public records requests within five business days. The response must either grant or deny the request, or provide a specific date by which the agency expects to be able to respond. || In New York, public records requests are governed by the Freedom of Information Law (FOIL). Under FOIL, agencies are required to respond to a request within five business days. The response must either grant or deny the request, or provide a reasonable estimate of the time it will take to comply.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
example_answers: Under New York’s Freedom of Information Law (FOIL), agencies must respond to a request for public records, including Medicaid records, within five business days after the request is received, unless an extension is granted by the agency. || Under New York’s Freedom of Information Law (FOIL), state agencies—including those that handle Medicaid requests—must respond to a public records request within five business days, either by providing the records, denying the request, or stating that additional time is needed. || Under New York’s Freedom of Information Law (FOIL), agencies – including the New York State Department of Health and its Medicaid offices – must respond to public records requests within five business days of receipt. If additional time is needed, the agency must provide a written notice of the extension and the reason for it.

question_template: In {state}, how much time do agencies have to respond to public records requests on {program}?
example_answers: Under Rhode Island law, agencies must respond to public records requests concerning Medicaid within five business days after the request is received, unless an extension is granted for a valid reason. || Rhode Island law requires state agencies, including the Department of Human Services (which processes Medicaid requests), to respond to a public records request within 20 business days of receiving the request. || Rhode Island state agencies must respond to public records requests within five (5) business days, as required by the Rhode Island Access to Public Records Act, and this includes requests for Medicaid records.
